entry.title: Re: Modernizing SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central Payroll
entry.content:
Not sure if I made it clear but we -are- on the S/4HANA version of EC Payroll, and I don't know about the older versions - but it should be more ready for Joule with the whole "Explain my Pay statement" thing, and of course HANA.

That said, we got our system nearly 2 years ago and I think in November there was a security note for HANA DB and we were like, shouldn't we upgrade this? This wasn't an option. After a long, long discussion after pointing out they promise to at least upgrade it once a year (it had been 1,5 year at this point) they finally upgraded it - explaining they wanted to test is properly. But it leaves a bit of a sour taste. You as a customer are supposed to take care of security notes that are not criticial (lower than CVSS score 9), but if you request it - they won't oblige.

Similarly the SAP router we bought with it, is on a version of 2024. We tried to request an update and where told plain and simple that this was not an option..

Guess it's still better than 7 year old stuff (I realised it was 6 years old when we were facing a decision so must be older now).

entry.title: Re: Clarification on Transport Management for Company Cod...
entry.content:
Dear @ASMAE3,

In Public Cloud there is no standard mechanism to deactivate, delete, or selectively exclude a confirmed Company Code from being transported across the landscape. Both the organizational-structure model in CBC and the transport handling in the 3-system landscape are designed in a way that makes this kind of per-Company-Code include/exclude approach not feasible. The recommended path is to keep the incorrect Company Codes in place, retire them from operational use and add the new, correct legal entities alongside them

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/doc/c38e987da8cf471f8888d703938f2ef1/SHIP3.0/en-US/SAPCentralBusinessConfiguration_ProjectExperience_en-US.pdf

It is worth distinguishing the project status:

  1. If the incorrect Company Code has not yet been confirmed in CBC this is the right moment to correct the organizational structure before deployment. The correction should be done in CBC before the organizational structure is confirmed and deployed to the target system
  2. If the incorrect Company Code has already been confirmed in CBC, it cannot be deleted or unassigned through the standard Set Up Organizational Structure process. Even if it has not yet reached all downstream systems this is no longer a normal delete and exclude from transport scenario
  3. If the organizational structure has already been deployed to the customizing tenant / downstream landscape the Company Code is part of the configuration baseline. A later transport with new Company Codes will not remove the old incorrect Company Codes. Public Cloud transport handling is transport-request based, not a business-level mechanism to include one Company Code and exclude another one. CBC transport requests also have to follow dependency and sequence rules so unwanted CBC content is not handled by simply skipping selected Company Codes

More details below:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3547695

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3482742

The recommended approach, assuming the incorrect Company Codes are already confirmed or deployed:

  • Create the corrected legal entities with new Company Code IDs in CBC, validate them, and move them through the standard CBC transport path. Adding new organizational units after the initial setup is a fully supported CBC scenario

More details below:

https://me.sap.com/notes/0003126190

  • Mark the incorrect Company Codes with the description DO NOT USE, block G/L account postings and restrict their usage in master data

More details below:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3138435

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3580500

  • Restrict access at the business-role level Company Code is supported as a restriction field in business roles so end users assigned to operational roles can be prevented from selecting the incorrect Company Codes

More details below:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/2513783

  • If the project requirement is that the incorrect Company Codes must not exist at all in the QAS/PROD landscape this needs to be raised with the SAP customer-facing representative before further deployment because it cannot be solved by standard configuration or by selective transport

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Re: Future-Dated Records on Terminated Employees: 1H 2026 Finally Closes a Long-Standing Gap.
entry.content:
Hi @shaktibarath, this is a great enhancement, but we cannot get it to work.

After setting the new "Delete Future-Dated Records After Termination" field in an Event Reason to Yes, and then attempting to terminate a user with a future record with that Event Reason, we are blocked by an error message (ex. "A Job Info record effective 01/06/26 (after your selected Termination Date) exists. Please modify or delete that record as appropriate before terminating this employee.").

I have opened case 593580/2026 with SAP support for this.

Also, it seems to me that your statement "For non-status-changing event reasons, the cleanup is automatically enabled and no action is required" disagrees with what is stated in 1H 2026 Release: Streamlining Data after Employmen... - SAP Community, which says "All future‑dated Job Information and Compensation Information records (including recurring pay components) with an event reason for which the setting is enabled are automatically removed" (emphasis added). Don't we need to set this new field to Yes in the Event Reasons we want to have removed after a Termination?

Best of all — both courses are completely free on learning.sap.com. There is no barrier between you and the skills you need.

entry.title: Re: Use of Pegging for Demand-to-Procurement Traceability...
entry.content:
Good day @MarkusDaum 

Thank you for your question.

For your exact requirement, there is an important distinction to take note of:

If you procure to unrestricted stock because of a supplier minimum order quantity, the purchase order is normally a stock receipt, not a demand-specific receipt. In that standard make-to-stock logic, the system does not create a fixed and audit-stable one-to-one relationship saying:

PO 123 covers Reservation A, PO 127 covers Reservation B, and PO 129 covers Reservation C.

The stock is planned and consumed at material/Plant/MRP area level. Therefore, the standard stock list or material coverage view can show the sequence of requirements and receipts, but it does not provide stable business traceability between a stock PO and one specific reservation.

That been said, it can be said that, the Public Cloud does not provide a stable one-to-one assignment showing exactly which purchase order covers which individual reservation for standard stock procurement scenario. If the purchase order is created to unrestricted stock because of a supplier minimum order quantity, the receipt is treated as stock at material/plant or MRP area level. The system can show receipts and requirements in sequence, but it does not create a permanent business link such as PO 123 equals Reservation A.

Pegging can provide better planning visibility, especially in advanced planning scenarios. However, dynamic pegging can be recalculated during planning runs, and you should not treat it as permanent traceability. Fixed pegging is more stable, but it should be used only where the planning design requires it and where the capability is available in the activated scope.

What I recommend is as follows:

  1. Make use of Monitor Material Coverage or Manage material Coverage to review receipt and demand sequence.        
  2. Confirm whether the PO is stock procurement or demand-specific procurement.
  3. If you require stable traceability, evaluate a demand-specific process such as account-assigned procurement, make-to-order stock, project stock, or another supported requirement-specific process.
  4. If you require advanced planning, first confirm your license and scope activation with your implementation partner or SAP.
  5. In Manage Product Mast Data, check the Advanced Planning section and resolve the system message about creating the plant as an SCM location.                                                                                                                                  
  6. Do not rely on dynamic pegging as an audit-stable PO-to-reservation assignment.
  7. You may consider raising an SAP Customer Influence if your requirement is mandatory and none of the standard processes fits your demand. Influence Opportunity Homepage - Customer Influence

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Re: How to redefine RDS based OData services?
entry.content:
@Abh_2 the method I have described in my blog post has been tried out for RDS based services that have to be extended via additional entities that itself are being added via RDS.

What I assume is that you just had a look at the code that my report generates and picked up the idea of providing an updated XML that contains an additional entry for an association.

This I have never tried and I don't have the time to look deeper into this topic.

However when using RDS additonal methods are being generated, especially the handling of the get_last_modfied( ) method which is called in case when RDS is used in method DEFINE_RDS_4().

So you would have in addition to redefine get_last_modified() to make sure that the changed metadata is being loaded.

Regeneration of a SEGW project that has been delivered by SAP is not a good idea since this is technially a modifcation since it is tried to regenerate classes in the SAP namespace. You should thus refrain from doing so.

  METHOD get_last_modified.
    " 1st step: call SADL with the extended XML
    TRY.
        DATA(lv_sadl_modified) = CAST cl_sadl_gw_model_exposure( if_sadl_gw_model_exposure_data~get_model_exposure( ) )->get_last_modified( ).
      CATCH cx_sadl_exposure_error.
        ASSERT 0 = 1.
    ENDTRY.

    " 2nd step: call default and integrate
    rv_last_modified = super->get_last_modified( ).
    IF rv_last_modified < lv_sadl_modified.
      rv_last_modified = lv_sadl_modified.
    ENDIF.
ENDMETHOD.

entry.title: How to create an Aging Analysis report using a User selected Key Date leveraging SAC Date difference
entry.content:
Problem definition:

In the area of reporting, for example in aging Reports there is always a requirement to calculate days between 2 dates like posting date and key date or order date and delivery date etc. In SAP Analytics Cloud generally this leads to a story defined calculation of type Date Difference. This calculated Measure definition supports the following options.

  • Granularity - This is the granularity of the date difference calculation. Supported values are Year, Month, Date, Hour, seconds, minutes.
  • Time A and Time B -  These are the 2 dates of which we want to calculate the difference between. They support Current Date, Current Timestamp and Date type dimensions of the model.
  • Dimension Context - This is the dimension context under which the difference is calculated.
  • Result Aggregation - How the difference should be aggregated. You can keep it to AVERAGE or change as per requirement.

This calculation works perfectly if you want to calculate difference between 2 existing date type dimensions, or one date type dimensions and current date/time. The calculation does not support any Input control for dimensions and hence there is no option to calculate date difference between a date dimension and a user selected key date.

Technical Solution:

We will try to build a workaround solution to calculate date difference between user selected date and Date dimension which can help us create aging report with key date as selection.

In SAC stories calculation editor, date difference calculation is done by defining a calculated measure of type Date difference and below is how the setup looks like.

The only supported option for 2 dates are Current Date, Current Timestamp and Date dimension of the model. No option for any input control selection.

We want to calculate date difference between Date dimension and user selected date.

  • Define User defined key Date selection

To capture the user selection for key date we will define a Date Input control of type range and will use custom calendar. This will give us a calendar picker to select date. 

The resultant date input control can be hidden from outline view and also disconnected from any widgets by keeping the below setting for  the Input control Linked Analysis. Its just a place holder input control with no filtering affect on any Tables or widgets.

The above will help me get the below for key date selection. ( still waiting for SAC to provide calendar pickers to select dates ( From/ To and date ranges, ....topic for another day)

Now we have created a mechanism to allow users to select key date. Our next task is to pass this key date selection to our calculation.

The date difference calculation only supports dimensions of type date. Lets build up a date dimension with our user selected key date. 

SAC supports custom dimensions. The issue is custom dimensions also do not support input controls or variables. Lets workaround this problem too.

  • Define a Script variable to be used in calculated measures

Define a script variable of type integer as shown below. This variable will store the integer pattern for selected date. If user selects 15 March 2024, it should store 15042024. We will define the logic to extract this in subsequent steps.

  • Define a Text Box and assign the dynamic text binding to the selected Key date

This will help us to read the selected key date and extract the integer pattern from it. 

Output: The user selection is assigned as shown below.

  • Define a logic to extract integer date pattern from user selected date (Mar 15, 2024)

 we will use some java scripting to derive the formatted date from above. We can assign the below code to a button which will help us calculate the value and also refresh the application.

// 1. Read selected Key Date

var seldate=Text_1.getPlainText(); // Text Box value with dynamic text binding

var monthName = seldate.substr(0, 3);
var day = seldate.substr(4, 2);
var year = seldate.substr(8, 4);

// 2. Map month name to number
var monthNum = "";
if (monthName === "Jan") { monthNum = "01"; }
else if (monthName === "Feb") { monthNum = "02"; }
else if (monthName === "Mar") { monthNum = "03"; }
else if (monthName === "Apr") { monthNum = "04"; }
else if (monthName === "May") { monthNum = "05"; }
else if (monthName === "Jun") { monthNum = "06"; }
else if (monthName === "Jul") { monthNum = "07"; }
else if (monthName === "Aug") { monthNum = "08"; }
else if (monthName === "Sep") { monthNum = "09"; }
else if (monthName === "Oct") { monthNum = "10"; }
else if (monthName === "Nov") { monthNum = "11"; }
else if (monthName === "Dec") { monthNum = "12"; }

// 3. Reorder to DDMMYYYY
var formattedDate = day + monthNum + year;

console.log(seldate);
console.log(formattedDate);

SV_KeyDate=ConvertUtils.stringToInteger(formattedDate); // Script variable assignment

console.log(SV_KeyDate);

Application.refreshData(); //Trigger Refresh after assigning value to variable

 

  • Create a calculated Measure using the Script variable define earlier (SV_KeyDate).

Use @ to read the script variable

  •  Create a calculated Dimension of type Measure based dimension using the above calculated Measure

Configure to use measure values as dimension members. Keep decimal as 0.

  • Define another calculated dimension using the previous calculated dimension.

TODATE(SUBSTRING([d/"MBD_KD"].[p/ID],0,2)+"-"+SUBSTRING([d/"MBD_KD"].[p/ID],2,2)+"-"+SUBSTRING([d/"MBD_KD"].[p/ID],4,4),"dd-mm-yyyy")

So, our scripting will assign 15032024 to script variable SV_KeyDate. Calculated Measure will be assigned this value. Next our first calculated dimension will get "15032024" as member id. Next we use TODATE() ( Syntax: ToDate("date_string";"input_format") ) function to create a date member "15-03-2024" using "15032024". The above formula creates the date member in correct format.

  • Define the Final Calculated Measure for Date difference

 Now the calculation editor allows us to select the previous defined calculated dimension as one of the date selections in Date difference calculation.

 

  • Final Result

Excel calculation of Days Difference

 key date
Date15/03/202410/05/2024
01/01/202474130
04/01/202471127
06/01/202469125
07/01/202468124
10/01/202465121
03/02/20244197
05/02/20243995
08/02/20243692
11/02/20243389

Limitations: 

Currently The story does not refresh automatically on change in Key Date selections. Button interaction has been leveraged to calculate the dimensions and the measures used in the calculation and also trigger the table refresh. This part could be improved with a scripting workaround.

Summary:

This way we can achieve calculating date difference calculation between a standard date dimension value and user defined date selection. 
If you think this post has helped you, please like and share your thoughts in the comments section below. Incase of any queries please ask the same in the comments section and I would be happy to answer the same.

Thanks for reading. I hope you enjoyed the blog.

Check the SAP Analytics Cloud topic page for more up to date information https://community.sap.com/topics/cloud-analytics

follow and read other informative blog posts on SAC browse the below link

https://blogs.sap.com/tags/67838200100800006884/

 

Regards

Nikhil

entry.title: Sap activate exam
entry.content:
I’d like to ask about how to take the SAP Activate exam. From what I’ve read, the rules have changed there are now two options: a conversation with an AI agent, or recording yourself and then submitting the recording for evaluation. I don’t know yet which option I’ll choose, but are the questions visible during the conversation with the AI agent? I mean, if I can see the questions in English, I’ll understand them better. I’m worried that I might not understand the agent’s words, but seeing the questions will make it clearer to me. English isn’t my first language, and I’m worried I might not understand the questions. Overall, I really regret that there aren’t more language options for the test; I hope you’ll expand the language selection someday. My second question is, if I decide to record the test, how many days after submitting the recording will the results be available?

BR

Małgorzata Półtorak

entry.title: Re: SAP Build Process Automation: Cannot add rules in Dec...
entry.content:
The plus sign is just an image. Use the "Create Rule" button which sits below the image. Scroll down in the window if you don't see it. After you create and save at least one rule, you will see the "Create Rule" button/link on the top right of the Rules tab.

Additional tips: It looks like you are configuring an SAP Fieldglass approval decision. If so, (1) ensure your output Name is "Response" and (2) the list checkbox is selected to the right of the output parameter.  You can reference the documentation and videos via the links below.

  • BTP Business Rules Service for SAP Fieldglass (PDF)
  • Integrating SAP Fieldglass and BTP SBPA for Enhanced Approval Workflows (VIDEO)
  • Integrating SAP Fieldglass and BTP SBPA for Workflow On-Page Validations (VIDEO)

Below is an image showing the "Create Rule" button on the "Rules" tab of the Decision artifact.

Below is an image of the desired format for the output parameter, with "Response" in the Name field and the List checkbox selected.

entry.title: Re: Webi: Unable to sort Cross Table break sections using...
entry.content:
Removing the default break sort may solve your issue.  But if not, here is a similar example you could follow:

I do not know what to call your 2nd column, but in my example I am using eFashion data and let's use "Category" as an example for that column. 

 

Add a new column to the left of your total column in the crosstable and add a variable that returns the sum or count of Category for each KMA Market

With eFashion data, this is what it would correspond to:

Category Cnt by Line   =  Sum(Count([Category]) In ([Lines])) 

In your case, it may be the Sum(Sum … rather than Sum(count    e.g. =Sum(Sum([CategoryTotal]) In ([KMA Market]))

Confirm that calculation context is working and that the KMA Market subtotal is matching the variable for each row. 

On the break properties of KMA Market, remove the "Apply sort":

This sort would override your preferred sort.

Now select the whole crosstable and add a  Descending sort on the variable.

You can now hide this column, as it is only needed for sorting purposes.

Results:
 

Let us know if this works for you,
~Roxanne

entry.title: Re: SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition and ECTR
entry.content:
Thanks for raising this topic, @christian_kokholm5a5d46  — it’s a very real dilemma.
 
You’re absolutely right: SAP Engineering Control Center (ECTR) and SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition are both positioned as strategic, yet today they do not align seamlessly for R&D‑heavy, CAD‑centric customers.

How SAP positions this today (fact‑based view)

  • SAP ECTR remains an on‑premise solution and is not supported with SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition.
  • SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition follows a strict SaaS / clean‑core model with limited support for tightly coupled, on‑premise engineering integrations.
  • As a result, there is no like‑for‑like ECTR replacement in the public cloud today.
This means customers with deep mechanical/electrical CAD integration requirements must make architectural trade‑offs, not just technical ones.

What we see as realistic paths today

Based on partner project experience, customers typically land in one of these patterns:

1) Stay on‑premise or move to private cloud (to preserve ECTR)

  • Chosen by engineering‑driven manufacturers where CAD‑PLM‑ERP integration is mission‑critical.
  • Provides continuity of ECTR‑based processes.
  • Comes at the cost of slower SaaS innovation cadence and higher lifecycle management effort.

2) Hybrid  or two-tier landscape (Public Cloud ERP + on‑premise ERP with ECTR)

  • Often used as a transition architecture, not a pure target state.
  • ERP core moves to public cloud, while engineering stays on‑premise.
  • Introduces integration complexity, possibly dual master data governance, and operational overhead.
  • Viable, but needs very clear ownership and long‑term intent.

3) Rethink engineering integration using cloud‑native services

  • Some customers explore public‑cloud PLM or document‑centric approaches with "looser" CAD integration.
  • Requires process redesign and acceptance of functional differences compared to ECTR.
  • This is not a technical migration, but a business transformation decision.
None of these options is trivial — which is exactly the challenge you describe.

How SAP envision this (reading between the lines, cautiously)

Without speculating on roadmap commitments, what is clear from SAP Cloud ERP principles and implementation project experiences is:
  • Public cloud is optimized for standardized, loosely coupled integrations, not deep CAD control.
  • Hybrid or two-tier scenarios are explicitly supported, but typically positioned as transitional or selective, not always as the end‑state for core engineering.
  • Customers with very advanced R&D requirements are often better served (today) by private cloud or hybrid / two-tier strategies, at least until cloud‑native engineering capabilities mature further.

Practical guidance

For partners advising R&D‑heavy customers, a few lessons learned stand out:
  • Be explicit early about what is not available in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition — especially around ECTR‑style integration.
  • Treat this as a Fit‑to‑Standard and scope decision in the Explore Phase of SAP Activate, not a late technical surprise (a recurring cloud project pitfall) [Success Guide for SAP Business Suite | Digital Asset Management]
  • Frame the decision as “process strategy first, deployment model second”, not the other way around.
  • If public cloud is the strategic target, plan for process simplification or decoupling, not technical equivalence.

Open and as yet unanswered

  • There is no published public‑cloud roadmap that confirms ECTR‑equivalent capabilities.
  • Customers who need ECTR‑equivalent capabilities should actively use Customer Influence to make the demand visible.
  • Until then, private cloud and hybrid / two-tier architectures remain valid and necessary for many engineering‑centric customers.
Summary
ECTR and SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition are both strategic — but not for the same engineering depth today. For R&D‑heavy customers, the choice is less about technology and more about which compromises are acceptable right now.
AI transparency note
This response was prepared with AI‑assisted drafting and validated against available SAP project experience material. Please always verify against current SAP documentation and roadmap publications before final implementation consideration.
 
If this answer helped, please mark it accordingly.

entry.title: Re: Application- Post Goods Receipt for Purchasing Document
entry.content:
Thanks for the question, Saloni.
 
In SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, the Post Goods Receipt for Purchasing Document Fiori app (App ID F0843) is documented primarily for posting goods receipt (GR) by purchasing document and (per the help) the key required location input is the storage location. The step-by-step procedure in SAP Help Portal for “Posting Goods Receipt for the Purchasing Document” explicitly mentions “Specify the storage location” but does not mention a manual storage bin (BIN) input field. [help.sap.com]

What you can try in the app (mobile-friendly)

Check View Settings / personalization
SAP documents that the Post Goods Receipt apps (including Post Goods Receipt for Purchasing Document – F0843) support View Settings to show/hide available columns and fields on the main page. So, on your mobile device, open the app and look for View Settings (or the initial “set View Settings” dialog when opening the app the first time) and see where the  “storage bin” column is available to add. [help.sap.com]

 

Why this happens (process perspective)

SAP’s Warehouse Management capability in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition is designed to manage warehouses “down to storage bin level.”
So if your requirement is to choose between GR-AREA-1 and GR-AREA-2 bins at posting time, you typically need to ensure you are using the process/app that supports bin-level warehouse execution. [help.sap.com]
Sources (SAP Help Portal)
  • Post Goods Receipt for Purchasing Document (App ID F0843) [help.sap.com]
  • Posting Goods Receipt for the Purchasing Document (procedure mentions storage location) [help.sap.com]
  • How to Personalize the View Settings in Post Goods Receipt Apps (includes F0843) [help.sap.com]
  • Warehouse Management overview (bin-level mapping) [help.sap.com]
  • App Extensibility: Post Goods Receipt for Purchasing Document [help.sap.com]
AI transparency note
This response was prepared with AI‑powered assistance and validated against SAP Help Portal documentation visible at the time of drafting. Please verify against your landscape's release and configuration.
 
If this response was helpful to you, please mark it accordingly. Thank you.

entry.title: Re: Can we please use the 'old' Custom Analytical Queries...
entry.content:
Thanks for raising this — this is a topic some projects run into.

Short answer

No, there is currently no supported way to re‑enable the “old” Custom Analytical Queries app for creating new queries in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition. SAP has intentionally consolidated query creation into “Custom Analytical Queries and Services”, and the older app is kept in a read‑only / compatibility mode for existing content.

What this means in practice

  • Custom Analytical Queries (old app)
    • Can still be used to display and consume existing queries
    • Creation of new queries is intentionally disabled
    • Not planned to be re‑opened for creation (design decision, not a bug)
  • Custom Analytical Queries and Services (new app)
    • Is the strategic app going forward
    • Supports query creation and OData exposure in one place
    • Some features from the old app (for example, certain aggregation options on individual elements) are not yet fully on par
This behavior is consistent with SAP’s general approach in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition: once a successor app is delivered, creation is moved there and the predecessor is not re‑activated.

Recommended approach

  1. Model with CDS / released views first
    In some cases, missing aggregation behavior can be addressed by:
    • Choosing a different released analytical CDS view as a base, or
    • Adjusting key figure semantics (if available) rather than relying on query‑level aggregation.
  2. Check if the missing aggregation is already planned
    SAP is incrementally closing functional gaps in Custom Analytical Queries and Services.
    Always cross‑check the roadmap on roadmaps.sap.com for Analytics and Extensibility in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition.
  3. Raise an Influence request (strongly recommended)
    If the missing aggregation options are critical for partner/customer scenarios:
    • Submit a request via Customer Influence
    • Clearly describe:
      • Which aggregation option existed in the old app
      • Why it cannot be modeled with released CDS views today
      • The business impact (e.g. Finance, Inventory, Management Reporting)
    This is the only supported channel to influence parity between the old and new apps.

Bottom line

Re‑enabling the old app for query creation is not supported and not expected.
The only sustainable path is:
  • Use Custom Analytical Queries and Services, and
  • Actively influence SAP to close the remaining functional gaps.
AI transparency note
This response was prepared with AI‑powered assistance and reflects current SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition behavior as observed in implementation projects. Always validate against your landscape’s exact release.
 
If this helps, please mark the answer accordingly. Thanks!

entry.title: How to add validation for a field in PR item in SAP S/4 HANA Public Cloud (Step by Step)
entry.content:
Introduction:

https://community.sap.com/t5/abap-blog-posts/how-to-add-custom-field-in-purchase-requisition-item-in-sap-s-4hana-public/ba-p/14373930

In our previous blog, we learned about how we can add a field using the custom field app in SAP Public Cloud. In there, we have added a field for the department. Now, the client has requested us to make this field mandatory because the user may have forgotten about it, even though there is a field for department, so if we make it mandatory, then it won't let the user save the PR unless they fill in the department field. We will use the custom logic app.

In this blog, we will cover :

> How to add validation for a field in SAP S/4 HANA Public Cloud using the CUSTOM LOGIC app

>Implement logic for that field and publish it.

This is fully aligned with SAP Public Cloud Extensibility

Let's dive into the following steps:

 Create Custom Logic

> Navigate to Custom Logic Tile

Create a new implementation

  • You need to search for a business context. Here, we choose "Procurement: Purchase Requisition Item" 
  • Then select a correct extension point, click on step 2

  • Insert a suitable name  

  • Then click on review and create 
  • Once it is created, you have to publish it, then go to the Open Code Editor

Now you see the editor screen where you can write your logic.

Code: 

FIELD-SYMBOLS <fs> TYPE mmpur_s_pr_fldsel.

READ TABLE fieldselection_table ASSIGNING <fs> WITH KEY field = 'YY1_DEPARTMENT_PRI'.
IF sy-subrc EQ 0.
<fs>-fieldstatus = '+'.
ENDIF.

Save and publish it.

Before Implementing Custom Logic

 

After Implementing Custom Logic

Conclusion:

Custom Logic app is safe to use, and with minimum effort, we can create validation for any field in any standard app if it is there, and it is upgrade-safe too. SAP is also providing sample Code with it, so that it will help with the coding part. One more thing, we have to create an object using custom logic, in the customising server rather than the development server, then we move it using the "Export Software Collection" App. Even if the implementation is not present in the custom logic app, you can create a script for the validation, which we will discuss about it later.

Note: Before implementing, please go through the documentation provided by SAP for that particular extension.

Please feel free to connect if you have any queries 

Happy learning 😀

entry.title: Re: Post Refresh activities in People Anlaytics - 1H 2026

Hello @vtamil 

 

If by “refresh” you are referring to the SAP SuccessFactors Half-Yearly Production Release scheduled for May 15–16, there is no action required from your end. All release-related activities will be taken care of, and you should experience minimal to no disruption.

However, if you are referring to an Instance Refresh, please follow the guidelines outlined in 2315083 - Instance Refresh for Reporting and WFA Products (WFA/Canvas/Advanced Reporting/Story Reports) - SAP for Me

 

Thanks,

Raghu

entry.title: Re: NOT ABLE TO CHANGE GL ACCOUNT
entry.content:
Hello @jerrysam,

Once the follow-on documents have been created, the master data used in them is “frozen.” Changes made to the material master are not automatically reflected in all subsequent documents. This is standard behavior and is also important and correct from an audit perspective.

To rectify the mistake in all documents now, it is important to work backwards through the process chain until the data can be updated in the purchase order. Afterwards, the documents must be reposted so that the new master data is reflected in them.

Best regards,

Kerisha

entry.title: GRC Tuesdays: What's New in SAP Global Trade Services - Manage Origin of Goods
entry.content:
As an importer or a domestic operator, accurately collecting and documenting the origin of the externally-procured product and batches is of the utmost importance since it determines the duties, possible trade preference options available, and import restrictions that apply to the shipment.

As a matter of fact, the Country and Region of Origin is a mandatory attribute for Customs treatment, duty assessment, and anti-dumping.

In many organizations, the non-preferential country of origin (COO) is a critical data point used in systems like SAP Global Trade Services for duty calculation, compliance checks, and documentation validation. However, this data is typically gathered and maintained in ERP systems by supply chain teams handling purchasing and sales flows.

This creates a fundamental issue: supply chain personnel often confuse country of origin with country of supply (where goods are shipped from). Since they are not the true owners of this data and are primarily focused on operational execution, COO is frequently entered incorrectly or without proper validation—simply as a mandatory field required for processing.

 The impact is significant. Incorrect COO data leads to:

  • Errors in duty calculations & preference determination
  • Inconsistencies in documentation (e.g. delivery notes, invoices)
  • Time-consuming manual checks and corrections

When discrepancies are identified, they are usually corrected within GTS by customs or compliance teams. However, these corrections are not synchronized back to the ERP, resulting in persistent data misalignment between systems.

At its core, the problem is one of misaligned ownership and responsibility. COO is a compliance-critical attribute that should be owned and governed by customs or GTS teams, yet it is currently maintained by supply chain users who lack the context and accountability to manage it accurately.

An effective solution would involve shifting ownership of COO to customs/GTS, along with better system integration to ensure consistency between ERP and GTS, reducing errors, manual rework, and compliance risk.

In this area, a new Manage Country/Region of Origin app has been delivered with SAP Global Trade Services, edition for SAP HANA 2025 Feature Pack Stack 01 released in April 2026.

Thanks to this new feature, organizations can:

 

Manage the non-preferential country and region of origin for externally procured products and/or batches

 

Rely on high transparency, by identifying the country at the good receipt level

 

Get automatic update of the country and/or region of origin and region of origin in feeder system

 

Integration with other modules from SAP Global Trade Services, edition for HANA such as preference and customs management

It is important to understand the distinction between non-preferential origin and preferential origin of a product.

Non-preferential origin refers to the country where a product is either wholly obtained or entirely produced. It can also be the last country where the product underwent substantial transformation. Minor or minimal processing carried out in another location does not usually change the product’s origin status.

Preferential origin, on the other hand, applies to products that meet specific criteria set out in a particular free trade agreement. These rules determine whether a product qualifies for reduced or zero tariffs under that agreement.

 

How does it work?

 

With the Manage Country/Region of Origin app, you can manage the country and region of origin for your externally procured products directly.

Provided the country (or region) of origin is available in either an import customs declaration, a long-term supplier declaration (LTSD) or an inbound delivery, it can be retrieved automatically and assigned to the product master data, and batch master data if applicable, in the feeder system. This information can still, of course, be manually overwritten should it be required.

The process starts in the feeder system where the good receipt information resides and is transferred to SAP GTS. There, the information relevant for country of origin is consolidated and analysed in the new app Manage Country/Region of origin and determination approach applied on a scenario logic.

 

Scenario 1 – the country of origin can be determined

 

 

If the country or region of origin is documented and the same is true for every good receipt, the product master data is automatically updated in the feeder system.

Illustration in SAP GTS:

The 2 source documents – here Long-Term Supplier Documents – both display “Spain” as country of origin and “Baleares” as region of origin, so origin information can be determined.

Note: a similar approach applies to batch-managed products with stock levels being considered into the determination to define the country of origin for each batch individually. Here, product master data won’t be updated though but can be manually maintained.

 

Scenario 2 – the country of origin can’t be determined

 

In case the origin information for a given product cannot be automatically established, SAP Global Trade Services will raise an alert. The user can then drill-down into the system to review the source data and investigate: is it due to multiple countries from various suppliers for the same product? Is it due to source documents missing? Etc. If the origin can be established, the user can manually maintain in the source information and correct or adjust the final origin.

Illustration in SAP GTS:

 

Want to learn more?

 

If this short summary wasn’t sufficient to satisfy your curiosity – and it most likely is the case, then I would suggest watching the recording of the SAP Global Trade Services, edition for SAP HANA 2025 FPS01 Release Overview session that was delivered a few weeks ago.

During this webinar, Volker Mohr from SAP GTS Product Management, Carmen Abelein and Torsten Stolz from SAP GTS Engineering, provided an update on the various enhancements delivered in this new version. Focusing on Preference Management, Customs Management, Product Master Data and of course, a deep-dive into Origin of Goods with various scenarios and a live demo.

 

What’s next?

 

As its name suggest, this is only the very first Feature Pack but many more enhancements are planned to come, including Feature Pack 2 planned for end of the year.

To be amongst the first to know what’s next, you have 2 options – which are not exclusive:

 

1. Register to the SAP Roadmap Update for International Trade Management Solutions session planned for June 23, 2026

 

2. Keep an eye on the SAP Road Map Explorer portal for SAP Global Trade Services

 

 

Want to influence the roadmap?

 

If you would like to help shape the future of the solution, then take part in the Continuous Influence session for SAP Global Trade Services, edition for SAP HANA. 

Voting for the next review cycle is open until end of June 2026.

Improvement requests are prioritized by the community through voting, so cast your votes for proposed enhancements important to you. Review of an Improvement Request by SAP Product Management will happen when the request has reached the minimum threshold of 20 votes or more from customers with a first review cycle planned in Q3 2026.

And if you are interested in learning more about SAP solutions for Governance, Risk, and Compliance, feel free to fill-in the demo request form!

 

Thank you, Prashanth Kaja – SAP GTS Customer Advisory for EMEA, for collaborating on this blog.

 

 

Frequently Asked Questions

 

  • What are the pre-requisites for “Origin of goods” integration with SAP S/4HANA

The prerequisites are mentioned in the SAP Note 3710399 - Origin of goods: Introduction of the function for managing the origin of goods

 

  • What versions of feeder systems support this?

This is supported by feeder system SAP S/4HANA 2021 private cloud edition or higher. SAP ECC is not supported.

 

  • Is the field COO now available in the long-term supplier declaration (LTSD) maintenance?

Yes

 

  • Batches on material level vs Batches at plant level, what is supported?

Currently batches at plant level are supported.

 

  • Will aggregation of long-term supplier declarations (LTSD) still be required? And will preference determination pick the origin from this new app?

Preference determination is independent from origin of goods and does not consider or origin. It depends whether the agreement and yes aggregation of long-term supplier declarations (LTSD) is still required for preference determination.

 

  • Does the stock data get synchronized with the origin that has been determined?

It depends on whether it is a batch-managed product or not. For batched managed products a report is available to update the stock, for non-batched managed product, stock is not considered.

 

  • Will the country of origin in the SAP S/4HANA product master be cleared in case the country is not unique?

It is customizable. It is possible to delete or leave it as-is until the user decides to manually intervene.

 

  • How can we maintain multiple origin countries for a single product ID?

This is only possible when batches are activated and used.

entry.title: Agentic AI in the Classroom: A Faculty Guide to Building AI Agents with Joule Studio
entry.content:
Intro

AI agents are becoming a standard part of how enterprise software works. They plan, retrieve information, call APIs, and execute multi-step tasks with a human-in-the-loop. For faculty teaching in business, information systems, and digital transformation programs, the practical question is straightforward: where can students experience this today, without any administrative or cost barrier?

The SAP Build basic trial offering a 30-days renewable access to SAP Joule Studio answers that question and comes at no cost. It offers structured, curated learning for building enterprise-grade AI agents using Joule Studio which is accessible to any student or faculty member in a browser, with no procurement or setup required. This post explains what the environment offers, how its guided Workbooks work, and how it can be brought into the classroom with minimal preparation.

For a cross-linked post covering tabular AI prediction with the SAP-RPT-1 Playground and how it connects to agentic workflows please see "Tabular AI Prediction in the Classroom: A Faculty Guide to SAP RPT-1 Playground."

Overview: what “agent building” means today and why it could be relevant for business schools and information systems students.

Agent building refers to the software engineering practice of developing autonomous, goal‑directed software entities that combine large language models (LLMs) with external tools, memory mechanisms, and control logic so they can perform complex tasks with limited human supervision.

Modern agent building draws on reinforcement learning and planning, retrieval‑augmented generation (RAG), and multi‑agent systems. Despite being vastly based on technical concepts and topics one can teach the essentials of AI-agents in a business context without going deep into theory.

Agentic AI is already being widely adopted in the sphere of process innovation, service design, and decision support, and it allows to:

  • Explore how AI impacts operations, finance, supply chain, and customer experience.
  • Prototype service journeys with automation workflows.
  • Evaluate governance, risk, and ethical considerations of AI in business.

Further in the blog post we intend to explore the no-cost options from SAP and SAP Build basic trial in particula that give both university faculty members and students enterprise‑grade experience without the need to procure SAP software access with a focus on design and hands-on experience. 

SAP Build basic trial

SAP Build is the low‑code development platform where students create applications, automate workflows, and orchestrate agent steps. It is the same platform enterprise teams use to extend SAP systems without writing traditional code.

SAP Build basic trial offers non-barrier access to SAP Build for a 30-day period and is more than an empty system landscape. Its most valuable feature for teaching purposes is its Workbooks, a set of guided, ready-to-use learning modules.

Workbooks are designed to be easy to navigate and serve following purposes:

  • workshop facilitation
  • self-paced learning.

Each Workbook walks a learner through a complete scenario in one of two modes:

  • Live tenant mode: The student executes the scenario step by step on a real, running BTP environment - not a simulation. They click, configure, and build in the actual product.
  • Guided experience mode: A pre-configured scenario is already set up, and the learner is walked through it with contextual explanations at each step. This is particularly useful when time is short or when students need a reliable baseline before they start customizing.

For a faculty member running a workshop, this means you can assign a Workbook, point students to the Build basic trial, and have the entire class working through the same scenario on live infrastructure. For students or learners working independently outside class, the same content functions as a self-paced learning path.

The Workbooks span core Build topics, including application development, process automation, integration, and AI agent building with Joule Studio. Students can build their first agent, define its role and audience constraints, connect it to a tool, and test its behaviour, all within a guided, recoverable environment.

Agentic AI experience within SAP Build basic trial: The agentic AI experience in  SAP Build basic trial is available through the Workbook “Create a Joule Agent with Joule Studio” which explains how to build an intelligent maintenance agent using Joule Studio that checks whether maintenance orders can proceed based on material stock availability. The scenario demonstrates how agentic AI can automate manual checks and support better maintenance planning decisions.

Public Resources on Agentic AI and SAP Joule Studio

The resources below have been selected to support faculty and students at different levels of familiarity from a first orientation to hands-on agent construction. They span include demos, step-by-step tutorials, community posts, and structured learning assets, all publicly available at no-cost and usable directly as pre-class reading, in-lab reference material, or independent follow-up.

Public SAP Agentic AI Learning Assets

CategoryResourceDescription

System experience

SAP Build basic trial

Offers a non-barrier access to SAP Build for a 30-day period and is more than an empty system landscape. Its most valuable feature for teaching purposes are its Workbooks, a set of guided, ready-to-use learning modules.

Demo

Build, Test and Deploy Agents with Agent Builder in Joule Studio

Public SAP demo video walking through agent creation, testing, and deployment in Joule Studio

Demo

Joule Studio (guided tour & Interactive Value Journey)

Browser-based experience that demonstrates how to design skills and agentic AI scenarios

Tutorials and missions

Joule Studio: Agent Builder – Base AI Feature (discovery center)

Introduces the Joule Studio agent builder, including availability as a Base AI feature and links to missions and setup guidance

Articles, community posts

Complete Joule Studio Resource Hub

This regularly updated asset includes official announcements, demos, tutorials, and learning resources for Joule Studio and agent builder

Articles, community posts

Agentic AI in SAP: From Joule Agents to the A2A Protocol

Community post outlining SAP’s roadmap toward agentic AI, including Joule Studio

Articles, community posts

Describes Joule’s evolution into an agentic AI platform and how custom agents can be built and extended

SAP Learning Assets

Introducing Joule Studio in SAP Build

 

Explore the core concepts and developer experience for creating Joule agents in Joule Studio and discover how to use Joule’s built-in AI assistance (“vibe coding”) to conversationally build and refine agents.

SAP Learning Assets

Implementing Joule across Your Organisational System Landscape

After completing this learning journey, you will be able to explain the Joule ecosystem and architecture, assess identity and access readiness, and establish a unified Joule instance on SAP BTP.

SAP Learning Assets

Getting Started with Joule for Business Users

After completing this course, you will be able to Learn how to apply effective prompting techniques, manage conversations, and use Joule across different business functions and SAP solutions to improve productivity and support better decision-making.

Suggested Course Activities: Agentic AI with Joule Studio

The activity below can be run as a standalone lab or as part of the four-activity course structure described in the interconnected post on RPT-1.

Suggested course structure leveraging SAP Build basic trial

Activity

Description

Result

Asset recommended

Orientation: What enterprise AI agents actually do

Students watch the SAP TechEd overview on agentic AI and the SAP Foundation Model demo before class. In the session, the instructor maps the two AI patterns:  tabular prediction (RPT-1) and agent orchestration (Joule Studio) , onto a single business process of their choice.

In-class group discussion: where in this process would you use which model, and why?

·  SAP TechEd: Unlocking Agentic AI with Joule Studio in SAP Build

·  Enhance Business Predictions with SAP Foundation Model and Knowledge Graphs

Lab 2: Agent building in Joule Studio

Students open the SAP Build Basic Trial and work through the Joule Studio Workbook in guided mode. They build a simple agent, define its role and audience constraints, connect it to a tool, and test its outputs against a defined business goal.

Documented agent design covering goal, permitted actions, prompts, and one identified failure mode with a proposed mitigation

SAP Build Basic Trial

Going further: For a more advanced course (combining agent orchestration with structured AI prediction where an SAP-RPT-1 model serves as a tool an agent can call) see the connected post "Tabular AI Prediction in the Classroom: A Faculty Guide to SAP-RPT-1 Playground" which includes the full four-activity course structure and synthesis exercise.

Conclusion

The SAP Build basic trial with its Joule Studio Workbooks is available today, at no cost, and requires no institutional agreement or technical setup to bring into a classroom. It offers faculty in business, information systems, and digital transformation programs an enterprise-grade AI environment that is immediately accessible, methodically flexible, and directly connected to what organizations are deploying right now.

The guided Workbooks and the publicly available learning assets listed above invite instructors to adapt the materials to their own context, student profile, and learning objectives. We encourage the academic community as part of the SAP University Alliances program and beyond to experiment with these assets, share the scenarios and methodological approaches they develop, and contribute to a growing body of practice around teaching Agentic AI in higher education — because we believe that the most valuable curriculum designs for this space will come from faculty who know their students and their domain, not from any single source.

Final note

If you plan to further explore to bring SAP to teaching please, visit SAP University Alliances AI topic page and read an article which elaborates on comprehensive Agentic AI story line complemented with enterprise architecture and process management perspective.

entry.title: Re: How to Ensure Sales Order Routing is Selected in MTO...
entry.content:
Configuration Options

1) Production Version Prioritization
Assign validity periods or usage indicators to production versions. Ensure PV for Sales Order Routing has higher priority or restricted usage (e.g., only for MTO).

Limitation: SAP standard does not differentiate routing selection as flexibly as BOM selection.

2) Advanced Variant Configuration (AVC)
AVC allows dependency rules to drive routing selection. You can define rules so that if a Sales Order Routing exists, it overrides the Super Routing. This is more robust but requires AVC setup and dependency maintenance.

Enhancement Options

1) BAdIs / User Exits

  • BAdI MD_PLDORD_POST: Can be used to adjust planned order data after MRP run, including routing assignment.
  • BAdI WORKORDER_UPDATE: Allows manipulation of routing assignment during order creation.
  • Userexit EXIT_SAPLCOVG_001 (routing determination): Can be leveraged to force Sales Order Routing selection.

2) Custom Logic
Develop a check such that if Sales Order Routing exists for the material/order, override routing assignment in the planned order. Automate via enhancement so users don’t need to manually adjust.

Having said the above, the recommendations would be

Short Term: Use a BAdI (MD_PLDORD_POST) to enforce Sales Order Routing when available. This is the quickest fix without major AVC redesign.

Medium Term: Revisit production version setup — separate PVs for MTO vs. VC scenarios, with clear usage restrictions.

Long Term: Implement AVC rules for routing determination to achieve sustainable automation and flexibility.

entry.title: Tabular AI Prediction in the Classroom: A Faculty Guide to SAP-RPT-1 Playground
entry.content:
Intro

Structured, tabular data: sales records, financial transactions, supplier metrics, HR data, remains the backbone of enterprise decision-making. Large language models are powerful, but they might be not the right tool for every prediction task on business data. SAP-RPT-1 is a relational pre-trained transformer designed specifically for this kind of structured reasoning, and it is available today for free through the SAP RPT-1 Playground.

For faculty teaching in business, analytics, information systems, and software development, the RPT-1 Playground provides an immediately usable lab environment: pre-built business scenarios, a bring-your-own-data option, and an intuitive interface. All that comes without any cost or necessity to draft an institutional agreement. This post explains what SAP-RPT-1 does, what scenarios are available, and how it can be brought into a course.

For a interlinked post covering agentic AI workflows with SAP Build Basic Trial and Joule Studio — and a four-activity course structure that combines both tools — see "Agentic AI in the Classroom: A Faculty Guide to Building AI Agents using Joule Studio."

Overview: where tabular AI prediction fits in the broader AI picture

Agent building refers to the software engineering practice of development autonomous, goal‑directed software entities that combine large language models (LLMs) with external tools, memory mechanisms, and control logic so they can perform complex tasks with limited human supervision.

Modern agent building draws on reinforcement learning and planning, retrieval‑augmented generation (RAG), and multi‑agent systems. Despite being vastly based on technical concepts and topics one can teach the essentials of AI-agents in a business context without going deep on theory.

RPT-1 is one of the tools available to illustrate a concept that often stays abstract in the classroom: agents are 'orchestrators' of multiple AI-related capabilities. An LLM handles reasoning and language whereas model like RPT-1 handles so-called tabular prediction. Placing both in the same course gives students a side-by-side comparison of when to use which kind of AI and how an agent decides to call one versus the other.

SAP RPT-1 Playground

SAP-RPT-1 ("Rapid One") is a relational, pre-trained transformer model designed to provide instant predictive insights from structured, tabular business data without extensive model training.

A RPT model is an AI system pre-trained on large, structured, relational datasets to learn common tabular data patterns. Unlike large language models that process text sequences, SAP-RPT-1 is designed to understand relationships in structured [tabular] data, such as sales records, financial transactions, supplier performance tables, or human resources metrics.

The six pre-built scenarios (see table below) are business-grounded, making them a perfect match for a business school audience. Students in the respective business discipline tracks can connect the model to something in their own domain, then ask insightful design questions or complete an agentic scenario. That perspective of SAP-RPT-1 viewed as a tool adapter inside an agent, rather than a standalone model bridges the technical content to the business-process thinking the course in the business school typically builds.

The free SAP RPT playground makes this immediately actionable. Students can configure a prediction scenario, upload their own CSV data, and evaluate model outputs in an interactive interface, all without any administrative or cost barrier. Used before or alongside the Joule Studio Workbooks in the SAP Build basic trial, it gives students two complementary hands-on experiences: structured data prediction in SAP-RPT-1, and agent orchestration in Joule Studio, both accessible with no-barrier in a parallel browser session (see a sample scenario of a course in the section below: Hands-On Learning with SAP-RPT-1 and Joule Studio: A Suggested Academic Course Structure).

As previously mentioned the SAP RPT playground allows for configuring prediction scenarios and evaluating model performance through an intuitive, interactive interface to test multi-class classification and numerical regression where one can opt out for SAP-provided sample scenarios (see table below):

SAP-RPT-1 Sandbox Pre-build Scenarios

Functional area

Scenario

Short description

Procurement

Equipment sourcing

Toy dataset to get you started. Estimate carbon footprint from equipment order details.

Customer service

Service ticket prioritization

Predicts the priority level of customer service tickets to accelerate support.

Finance

Payment delay prediction

Prediction of the number of days payments are late to forecast cash flow, reduce risk.

Sales & Distribution

Sales order completion

Complete sales order details by predicting value of missing fields (Incoterms, Sales Group, ...)

Digital Supply Chain

Predictive maintenance

Uses historical equipment data to predict remaining useful life to optimize maintenance.

Customer Relationship Management

Offers recommendation

Recommendation of the next-best offer to boost revenue.

Alternatively, one can also follow a “bring your own data” approach and upload data through upload functionality and a CSV file. For clearer expectations the students need to be informed upfront about limits for CSV file:

  • Maximum rows: 2,073 rows per file
  • Maximum columns: 50 columns per file
  • Maximum cell length: 1,000 characters per cell
  • Maximum column name length: 100 characters

 

 

 

 

 

 

It’s also worth mentioning that an open-source version, sap-rpt-1-oss, is available on Hugging Face and GitHub for research and non-commercial purposes.

Public Resources on SAP RPT-1

The following resources cover SAP-RPT-1 from multiple angles and reflect the range of ways the model can be introduced in a course, whether as a standalone lab exercise or as context for a broader discussion on how structured prediction fits into an agentic workflow. All items are publicly accessible with no account or license required.

Public resources for SAP-RPT-1

Category

Resource

Description

System experience

SAP-RPT-1 Playground

This is a demonstration platform allowing to choose from pre-built data sets or upload your own CSV data to get instant predictions and insights powered by SAP's advanced machine learning models.

System experience

SAP-RPT-1 Playground

This is a demonstration platform allowing to choose from pre-built data sets or upload your own CSV data to get instant predictions and insights powered by SAP's advanced machine learning models.

Demo

Run AI predictions on live SAP data with SAP HANA Cloud

Learn how to run real-time Al forecasting directly inside SAP HANA Cloud using SAP's foundational model, SAP-RPT-1, no model training or data movement required.

Demo

Enhance Business Predictions with SAP Foundation Model and Knowledge Graphs

Discover how SAP Foundation Model, a table-native AI model, can accelerate time to value for predictive tasks on tabular data, addressing the limitations of large language models in business forecasting.

Community Blog Post

SAP-RPT-1, Document AI, Perplexity in AI Core

A use-case of adopting AI for “Onboarding of a new vehicle to the car fleet’ process decomposed from the perspective of SAP AI tools used.

 

Suggested Course Activities: Tabular AI Prediction with RPT-1

The activity below can be run as a standalone lab or as part of the four-activity course structure that combines RPT-1 with Joule Studio.

Suggested course structure with SAP-RPT-1 Lab

Activity

Description

Result

Asset recommended

Orientation: What enterprise AI agents actually do

Students watch the SAP Foundation Model demo before class. In the session, the instructor contrasts structured prediction (RPT-1) with language-based reasoning (LLMs) and maps both onto a single business process.

In-class group discussion: how RPT’s approach is different from LLM?

Enhance Business Predictions with SAP Foundation Model and Knowledge Graphs

Lab: Structured prediction with SAP-RPT-1

Students access the SAP-RPT-1 Playground, run two of the pre-built scenarios, and evaluate the outputs. They then upload a small dataset of their own or from a provided sample.

One-page brief identifying the prediction task, the input data used, the output quality, and one business decision the prediction would inform.

SAP-RPT-1 Playground

 

Going further: For a more advanced course arc that combines structured AI prediction with agent orchestration and where an SAP-RPT-1 prediction becomes a tool call inside a Joule agent, below is the integrated structure that includes both environments. The overarching goal is to move students from AI literacy to AI design ability which is not the ability to train models or write infrastructure code, but rather the ability to reason about which AI-capability to apply to which business problem, how to design those capabilities into an automated workflow, and how to evaluate the outcome (for the part covering in more detail usage of the SAP Basic Trial in academic teaching please refer to the blog). The format is based on experiential, problem-based learning and the four-activity structure follows a deliberate pedagogical arc.

Suggested course structure leveraging SAP Build Basic Trial and SAP-RPT-1

Activity

Description

Result

Asset recommended

Orientation: What enterprise AI agents actually do

Students watch the SAP TechEd overview on agentic AI and the SAP Foundation Model demo before class. In the session, the instructor maps the two AI patterns: tabular prediction (SAP-RPT-1) and agent orchestration (Joule Studio), onto a single business process of their choice.

In-class group discussion: where in this process would you use which model, and why?

  • SAP TechEd: Unlocking Agentic AI with Joule Studio in SAP Build
  • Enhance Business Predictions with SAP Foundation Model and Knowledge Graphs

Lab 1: Structured prediction with SAP-RPT-1

Students access the SAP-RPT-1 Playground, run two of the pre-built scenarios, and evaluate the outputs. They then upload a small dataset of their own or from a provided sample.

One-page brief identifying the prediction task, the input data used, the output quality, and one business decision the prediction would inform.

SAP-RPT-1 Playground

Lab 2: Agent building in Joule Studio

Students open the SAP Build basic trial and work through the Joule Studio Workbook in guided mode. They build a simple agent, define its role and audience constraints, connect it to a tool, and test its outputs against a defined business goal.

Documented agent design covering goal, permitted actions, prompts, and one identified failure mode with a proposed mitigation

SAP Build basic trial

Synthesis: Design an integrated agent scenario

Teams present a business scenario in which an SAP-RPT-1 prediction feeds into a Joule agent as a tool call. Neither coding nor hands on required; the focus is on design thinking and responsible AI practice.

Resulting presentation must cover the data flow, the decision logic, the governance checkpoints, and how success would be measured.

SAP-RPT-1, Document AI, Perplexity in AI Core

Conclusion

The SAP RPT-1 Playground is available today, at no cost, and requires no account, no institutional agreement, and no technical setup to bring into a classroom. It gives students in analytics, operations, finance, and information systems programs direct hands-on experience with a production-grade tabular AI model across six pre-built business scenarios, plus the “bring-your-own-data” mode supports instructors who want to tailor the lab to their own domain dataset.

The pre-built scenarios, the open-source availability of SAP-RPT-1 on Hugging Face and GitHub, and the publicly available demo and community materials all invite instructors to adapt and extend. We encourage the academic community as part of the SAP University Alliances program and beyond to experiment with these assets, share the scenarios and methodological approaches they develop, because we believe that the most valuable curriculum designs will come from faculty who know their students and their domain, not from any single source.

Final note

If you plan to further explore AI topic with SAP for teaching, please visit SAP University Alliances topic page and read an article which elaborates on comprehensive Agentic AI story complemented with enterprise architecture and process management perspective.

entry.title: Adding Custom Sort Metrics in SAP Commerce
entry.content:
Introduction

In modern e-commerce, providing customers with flexible sorting options is crucial for enhancing user experience and driving sales. While SAP Commerce provides default sorting options like relevance, price, and name, businesses often need custom sorting criteria that align with their specific merchandising strategies. This blog demonstrates how to add a "sold amount" sorting metric to an electronics store, allowing customers to discover popular products based on actual sales performance.

This implementation targets SAP Commerce developers and solution architects who need to extend the platform's search and navigation capabilities. Prerequisites include familiarity with SAP Commerce architecture, Solr configuration, and basic Java development.

Understanding Current Sort Implementation

SAP Commerce provides several out-of-the-box sorting options including relevance (default), top rated, name (ascending/descending), and price (lowest/highest first). The sorting functionality is built on Solr Facet Search, where sort options are configured through SolrSort and SolrSortField entities.

The technical architecture involves:

  • SolrIndexedProperty: Defines searchable/sortable product attributes
  • SolrSort: Represents the sort option visible to customers
  • SolrSortField: Maps sort options to specific Solr fields
  • Storefront Integration: Displays sort options in the product listing pages

Sort configurations are typically managed through the Backoffice under System → Search and Navigation → Solr Facet Search Configuration.

Step-by-Step Implementation

Step 1: Define the New Sort Option

First, create the Solr sort configuration using ImpEx. This defines how the "sold amount" sorting will appear to customers:

$solrIndexedType=electronicsProductType

 

# Define the available sorts
INSERT_UPDATE SolrSort;indexedType(identifier)[unique=true];code[unique=true];useBoost
         ; $solrIndexedType;soldAmount-asc;false
         ; $solrIndexedType;soldAmount-desc;false

# Update the available sorts for i18n
UPDATE SolrSort;indexedType(identifier)[unique=true];code[unique=true];name[lang=en]
               ;$solrIndexedType;soldAmount-asc;"Sold Amount (ascending)"
               ;$solrIndexedType;soldAmount-desc;"Sold Amount (descending)"

# Define the sort fields
INSERT_UPDATE SolrSortField;sort(indexedType(identifier),code)[unique=true];fieldName[unique=true];ascending[unique=true]
            ;$solrIndexedType:soldAmount-asc;soldAmount;true
            ;$solrIndexedType:soldAmount-desc;soldAmount;false

 

Step 2: Implement Backend Logic

Create a field provide to calculate the sold quantities with a fake algorithm (get the value from product code or PK):

public class SoldAmountProvider extends AbstractPropertyFieldValueProvider implements FieldValueProvider
{
    private FieldNameProvider fieldNameProvider;

    @Override
    public Collection<FieldValue> getFieldValues(final IndexConfig indexConfig, final IndexedProperty indexedProperty,
          final Object model) throws FieldValueProviderException
    {
       final ProductModel productModel = (ProductModel)model;
       if (productModel == null)
       {
          return Collections.emptyList();
       }

       final Collection<FieldValue> fieldValues = new ArrayList<FieldValue>();
       final String code = productModel.getCode();
       long soldAmount = 0;
       try {
          soldAmount = Long.parseLong(code);
       } catch (NumberFormatException nfe) {
          String pkString = productModel.getPk().toString();
          int length = pkString.length();
          soldAmount = Long.parseLong(pkString.substring(length-5));
       }

       fieldValues.addAll(createFieldValue(soldAmount, indexedProperty));
       return fieldValues;
    }

    protected List<FieldValue> createFieldValue(final long soldAmount, final IndexedProperty indexedProperty)
    {
       final List<FieldValue> fieldValues = new ArrayList<FieldValue>();
       final Collection<String> fieldNames = fieldNameProvider.getFieldNames(indexedProperty, null);
       for (final String fieldName : fieldNames)
       {
          fieldValues.add(new FieldValue(fieldName, soldAmount));
       }
       return fieldValues;
    }

    public void setFieldNameProvider(FieldNameProvider fieldNameProvider) {
       this.fieldNameProvider = fieldNameProvider;
    }
}

 

Step 3: Configure Bean Definition

Configure a bean definition for the above field provider to be used in the Solr indexer property:

<bean id="soldAmountProvider"
      class="org.training.core.search.solrfacetsearch.provider.impl.SoldAmountProvider"
      parent="abstractPropertyFieldValueProvider">
    <property name="fieldNameProvider" ref="solrFieldNameProvider"/>
</bean>

 

Step 4: Configure Solr Indexing

Configure Solr to index the sold quantity data for sorting purposes [1]:

$solrIndexedType=electronicsProductType

INSERT_UPDATE SolrIndexedProperty;solrIndexedType(identifier)[unique=true];name[unique=true];type(code);sortableType(code);currency[default=false];localized[default=false];multiValue[default=false];useForSpellchecking[default=false];useForAutocomplete[default=false];fieldValueProvider
         ;$solrIndexedType; soldAmount                   ;long   ; long ;    ;    ;                                                                       ;   ;   ;soldAmountProvider 


# Declare the indexed type Product
INSERT_UPDATE SolrIndexedType;identifier[unique=true];type(code);variant;sorts(code)
       ;$solrIndexedType;Product;false;relevance,topRated,name-asc,name-desc,price-asc,price-desc,soldAmount-asc,soldAmount-desc

 

 

Step 5: Testing and Validation

Implement comprehensive testing to ensure the sorting functionality works correctly:

  1. Unit Tests: Verify service layer calculations
  2. Integration Tests: Test Solr indexing and search results
  3. Performance Tests: Assess impact on page load times
  4. User Acceptance Tests: Validate sorting accuracy on storefront

After configuration changes, perform a full Solr reindex to ensure the new sorting data is available.

 

Troubleshooting Common Issues

Index Rebuild Requirements: After adding new sort fields, always perform a full Solr reindex. Navigate to System → Facet Search → Index Operations in Backoffice.

Cache Invalidation: Clear relevant caches after configuration changes, particularly the search result cache and product catalog cache.

Performance Optimization: For large catalogs, consider implementing incremental updates for sold quantities rather than full recalculation. Use database indexing on the soldQuantity field for better query performance.

Data Accuracy: Implement proper transaction handling when updating sold quantities to ensure data consistency across concurrent operations.

Conclusion

Adding custom sorting metrics like "sold amount" significantly enhances the customer shopping experience by surfacing popular products based on actual sales performance. This implementation demonstrates SAP Commerce's flexibility in extending search and navigation capabilities while maintaining system performance.

The sold amount sorting feature provides immediate business value by helping customers discover trending products and enabling merchandisers to leverage sales data for better product positioning. Consider extending this approach with additional metrics like profit margins, seasonal trends, or customer ratings for even more sophisticated sorting strategies.

For additional guidance on search configuration and custom sorting implementations, refer to the SAP Commerce documentation on Solr Facet Search and explore the SAP Community for advanced use cases and best practices.

entry.title: When displaying the search help for BP, is it possible to set default value "C" to the initial value
entry.content:

entry.title: SAP LeanIX Academic Edition Relaunch: Bringing Enterprise Architecture into the Classroom
entry.content:
We are excited to announce the relaunch of the SAP LeanIX Academic Edition, giving lecturers free access to industry-leading Enterprise Architecture (EA) software for teaching and research.

Why It Matters

Enterprise architecture is essential for managing today’s complex IT landscapes and driving digital transformation. With SAP LeanIX, lecturers can move beyond theory and provide students with hands-on experience using real-world tools.

What the Academic Edition Offers

  • ✅Dedicated LeanIX workspace
  • ✅Hands-on learning with real EA scenarios
  • ✅Up to 100 applications for modeling IT landscapes
  • ✅Hosted in the EU for reliable access
  • ✅Free for verified lecturers

Students can explore key EA such as:

  • Application portfolio assessment
  • IT landscape analysis
  • Risk and dependency identification

Part of the SAP Ecosystem

SAP LeanIX plays a central role in SAP’s enterprise transformation strategy, helping organizations:

  • Align business and IT
  • Navigate SAP S/4HANA transformations
  • Manage application landscapes and modernization

Teaching with LeanIX gives students direct insight into how leading companies design and transform their IT architectures within the SAP ecosystem.

How to Get Started

Lecturers can apply via SAP Learning Hub, lecturer zone via the following steps:

Step 1: Apply for SAP Learning Hub, Student Edition (LHSE)

  • Lecturers must first request access to the SAP Learning Hub, Student Edition (LHSE)
  • Apply via the official LHSE landing page using your academic email address
  • Once approved, you will have an active LHSE subscription

Step 2: Request Access to LeanIX Academic Edition

  • After your LHSE subscription is active, you can request access to SAP LeanIX Academic Edition
  • Use the dedicated request button (link to be inserted) available within the program
  • Important: Only lecturers can request system access

Step 3: Get Verified and Start Using LeanIX

  • After verification, you will receive access to your dedicated LeanIX workspace
  • As a lecturer, you can then:
    • Set up your course environment
    • Add and manage your students

Important Note for Student Access

  • Students cannot request access directly
  • Access is granted only through lecturers

Start Teaching with Impact

With the SAP LeanIX Academic Edition, you can bring real enterprise architecture practice into your classroom and prepare students for careers in digital transformation.

👉Apply now and empower the next generation of enterprise architects
