entry.title: AI Agent Skills and their impact on SAP business processes
entry.content:
As usually happens to a consultant that works with SAP, business problems come to my desk. "This process doesn't work", "We can't work with this". 3 workshops later we have the usual solution, enhance a report, some extra columns while business swears X = Y only to be later confronted with X = Z. The usual. Wouldn't it be nice if business rules were managed by end users instead of us having to configure and code? That's what agent Skills (e.g Claude Skills) can do for you. 

This particular example deals with an export process, where we have the customer revenue but also a lot of service pro forma/invoices for port, terminal and custom costs which from a profitability perspective need to be posted to the same "process" as the revenue. Lots of supplier, different document layouts, all of them need to be matched to sales orders/deliveries. 

  • "The user" Solution - A business user looks at each pro forma, interprets it, goes into SAP and manually picks the sales orders/delivery items that the cost refers to, distributes the cost to each item and then creates a purchase order for it. Result: the user complains and says "I need 1 more FTE for this". 
  • "The custom" Solution - We develop a sophisticated system to interpret the pro forma, try to find what the items mean, where the reference to our process is, and then automate the distribution. Result: Each time we have a new invoice type or supplier we have to at least test, maybe develop a new exception and ... we need one more IT FTE (I'm joking, it's just extra work). 
  • "The AI" Solution - We give Claude to a user. He uploads the pro forma and asks Claude to convert to a purchase order format. He tells Claude what to do for that supplier, and what to do with the references, even how he wants to distribute the cost. And he finishes by saying "save this as an extract quote skill". Next time? Just ask upload and ask him to create a purchase order. Do you have another supplier? Tell them what to do with this one, and ask it to add the new behaviour particular to this vendor to the skill. 

Looks like magic right? But the most "magical" (or maybe concerning) thing about this is that there is no ABAP, no Fiori, no configuration. Just an AI Agent using MCP + OData to interact with a standard SAP system. To prove this not a magic scenario, look at this demo where I go through this very example: 

 

 

So I will just leave you with a call to action: Think about examples like this and how it will impact the way we do business processes and the skills that will become more relevant for functional consultants

Do I think there is still room for SAP consultants in the future? Of course, but I truly believe the role will become more and more a true consultant on business processes and less a producer of functional specifications.

And maybe, just maybe, we will finally have clean core.

entry.title: Three Ways for Academia to Access SAP Signavio - Plus: Retirement in 2026 and What’s Coming Next
entry.content:
Three Ways for Academia to Access SAP Signavio - Plus: Retirement in 2026 and What’s Coming Next

SAP continues to strengthen its commitment to business process transformation education. With a redesigned academic offering, universities, lecturers, and students will benefit from broader access to the full SAP Signavio capabilities, more flexible access models, and a clear long-term roadmap aligned with real-world practice.

As part of this evolution, we want to share an important update early and transparently:
the current self-service SAP Signavio academic offering will be retired in 2026 to make way for a more powerful and future-ready academic setup.

In this post, we walk you through what’s changing, when those changes will take place, and how you can prepare so you can continue teaching and learning without disruption while taking advantage of what’s coming next.

 

What’s Changing – and When?

The current SAP Signavio Process Transformation Suite, academic edition (legacy self-service instance) will be phased out by September 30, 2026. It will be replaced by a functionally more capable academic offering including the full SAP Signavio feature set, including SAP Signavio Process Intelligence capabilities.

Key Milestones of the legacy instance phase-out:

  • New Academic Edition available: May 30, 2026
  • New registrations for the legacy instance end: July 31, 2026
  • Legacy academic instance fully retired: September 30, 2026
  • Data available for export: As defined in the applicable Terms & Conditions

You can continue using the current Academic Edition without interruption until September 30th, 2026.
Before this date, please export any models, assignments, or coursework you want to keep (BPMN, PDF, or CSV formats recommended)

The New Academic Offering: Three Access Models

Going forward, universities can choose from three complementary access models, each designed for different educational needs and teaching styles. These models do not replace one another but instead offer flexibility across institutions and learners.

Model 1: Direct University Access – Free Academic Edition

Full SAP Signavio. Zero Cost. University-Controlled.

This is the new route for universities seeking direct, no-cost access.

What’s Included

  • Full SAP Signavio Process Transformation Suite
    (Process Manager, Journey Modeler, Process Insights, Process Intelligence, and more)
  • Dedicated tenant per university
  • Suitable for teaching and research
  • Basic example content for initial orientation

Usage Scope

  • Up to 100 users per university
  • Up to 10,000 process analysis records per user per year

How It Works

  • A direct contract between SAP and the university
  • The university is fully in control:
    • Managing users
    • Governance
    • Solution setup
  • Ideal for institutions wanting straightforward platform access without a need for a structured curriculum in addition to system access, ready-to use teaching materials or related support services

Best for universities that prefer autonomy and flexibility with full product capabilities.

Interested?
📧Contact us at signavio.academics@sap.com

 When reaching out by email, please include:

  • First name
  • Last name
  • Educational institution
  • Academic role at the institution

Model 2: Full Academic Experience via UCC (Extended Services)

Structured Learning with Guidance and Integration

For universities that prefer a fully tailored academic experience, SAP continues to work closely with trusted partners such as University Competence Center (UCC) at the Technical University of Munich.

What’s Included

  • Ready-to-use curricula and exercises
  • Managed system access
  • Train-the-trainer sessions
  • Advisory and support services
  • Optional integration with SAP systems (for example, SAP S/4HANA)
  • Additional learning materials

What makes this offering stand out is the integration with SAP S/4HANA. The data structure is synchronized, which means students can do process mining on a live ERP system with real transaction and master data across module boundaries. They are not working with isolated sample files. They see how processes actually behave across procurement, finance, logistics, and other areas in one connected system.

"The University Competence Center Munich has worked with SAP for many years, and SAP Signavio has long been part of what we offer to lecturers and students. What is new now are the expanded capabilities. With the curriculum Introduction to Process Mining using SAP Signavio Process Intelligence, universities can not only access the full suite directly, but also benefit from a structured, fully tailored academic experience including curriculum, teaching materials, enablement, and support services. Made from lecturers for lecturers."

Dr. Holger Wittges, Managing Director SAP University Competence Center, Technical University of Munich

How It Works

  • The University Competence Center serves as the main contact. Please contact your local University Competence Center (UCC) or Academic Competence Center (ACC) for further information and guidance, or directly at the University Competence Center at the Technical University of Munich via support@ucc.tum.de.

👉Best for universities looking for comprehensive course content, guided onboarding, and additional services.

Model 3: Practice System / Sandbox via SAP Learning Hub, student edition

Hands-On Access for Individual Learners for Process Modeler

For students and lecturers working independently (for example on theses, projects, or self-study) we are expanding free access via SAP Learning Hub, student edition.

Capabilities

  • Sandbox-style SAP Signavio system for process modelling
  • Individual, hands-on practice environments
  • No institutional contract required

This option provides a low-friction entry point into SAP Signavio and is ideal as a complement to university courses.

Interested?
👉Access the SAP Signavio sandbox directly via the SAP Learning Hub, student edition.

What Lecturers and Students Should Do Now

For Lecturers

  • Finish current courses using the existing academic offering
  • Export any content you will need beyond September 30, 2026
  • Interested in the new extended SAP Process Transformation Suite, academic edition already?
    Reach out to signavio.academics@sap.com  

For Students

  • Continue using SAP Signavio for current coursework as normal
  • Export personal work before retirement on September 30, 2026
  • 🗣Inform your lecturer - If you’re using SAP Signavio for a thesis, independent research, or projects, notify your lecturer—access is now coordinated through your university, so your lecturer will need to arrange it on your behalf.
  • 🎓Follow your university’s guidance for enrollment once the new offering is live, or alternatively use the sandbox system in SAP Learning Hub, student edition, for process modeling.

Migration & Export Guidance

Detailed step-by-step documentation on exporting data from the legacy academic edition and importing it into the new environments will be provided ahead of retirement.

Looking Ahead

With these new academic offering, SAP delivers a more capable, scalable, and future-proof learning experience, supporting universities, educators, and students alike.

We will additional updates, FAQs, and onboarding guidance to help you plan the transition.

Questions or ready to explore your options?
📧signavio.academics@sap.com

We look forward to supporting the next chapter of process transformation education with SAP Signavio.

entry.title: No more black boxes: Achieve Full Process Visibility with SAP Build Process Automation and SAP IPD
entry.content:
Welcome to this blog post! In today's edition, I want to outline how companies can achieve full end-to-end process visibility using SAP Build Process Automation (SBPA) integrated with SAP Integrated Product Development (IPD) to move beyond manual status checks and fragmented inboxes.

No time to read? Register to access the recording of our latest Meet the Expert webinar via the SAP Supply Chain Management Enablement Hub here.

End-to-end process visibility is not a reporting exercise; it’s the basis for eliminating revenue‑draining bottlenecks across product and supply chain operations. Status fields and ad hoc check‑ins create an illusion of control while fragmentation persists between systems, teams, and partners. The consequences are measurable: longer cycle times, governance gaps, compliance exposure, and poor supplier and stakeholder experiences.

In this blog, you will find out how manufacturing and industrial companies can:

  • Design and automate workflows using low-code/no-code tools in SAP Build Process Automation
  • Gain real-time, graphical visibility into running processes and identify bottlenecks proactively
  • Manage all workflow tasks from a single unified inbox using SAP Task Center
  • Leverage AI to generate workflows, forms, and first-level task analysis

 

The Challenge: Visibility Gaps Are Costly

Most teams know they have bottlenecks – the challenge is finding them in time. Product teams often face a dual mandate: move faster and stay compliant across functions and partners. The fastest path is to bring workflows, tasks, and product data into one place and layer AI on top to remove friction. When every task carries its linked files, reference objects, and context, teams can stop chasing information and start executing with clarity.

Our webinar poll confirmed it: confidence in current KPIs actually reflecting real process bottlenecks is low. The symptoms are familiar: too many "what's the status?" calls, siloed systems, disconnected departments, and compliance risks lurking beneath the surface.

What is needed is a connected workflow layer that makes every process step visible, traceable, and actionable in real time.

 

The Solution: Three Capabilities That Make the Difference

1. Design Workflows visually and with AI Assistance

SAP Build Process Automation provides a low-code/no-code environment for building workflows visually. You can leverage AI to generate business processes based on your inputs in natural language.

[An image that shows the Generate button.]

The same applies to forms – describe the fields you need and AI builds them, complete with correct field types and validation rules.

[An image that shows the graphical path analysis.]

Unify workflow orchestration with a visibility layer. Use low-code automation to model cross-functional processes (collaboration tasks, approvals, document exchanges, and system updates) while turning each process instance into interactive analytics. Provide path analysis, cycle times, backlog by assignee or role, SLA thresholds, and real-time bottleneck detection. When a step is delayed due to missing input, highlight the issue, quantify impact, and trigger corrective or preventive actions. With full context on every task, you can instantly drill-down from portfolio-level KPIs to the specific artifact or decision causing the delay. Connect cloud and legacy systems, internal teams, and external partners to deliver complete, actionable audit trails.

Make visibility prescriptive to drive business impact. Baseline metrics like cycle time, throughput, and trends are useful, but value accelerates when thresholds and alerts match operational realities like supplier qualification lead times, label review SLAs, engineering change approval windows, best-before or delivery-date risk. Anchor KPIs in system data instead of spreadsheets to cut coordination meetings, eliminate status chasing, and push decisions to the point of work. Organizations report double-digit cycle time reductions by replacing manual checks and vague “in progress” statuses with orchestrated tasks, unified action inboxes, and auto-generated insights that surface risks before they escalate.

2. Execute Tasks from a Unified Inbox

A unified Task Center, paired with process visibility and AI guidance, turns workflows into an execution cockpit: one place to see blockers, ownership, standards, and fixes.

All workflow tasks route directly to SAP Task Center – a single inbox connecting SAP IPD, SAP S/4HANA, SAP Ariba, and other systems. Users can claim tasks, review linked product data, fill in forms, and submit approvals without switching between systems. The result is a resilient, auditable digital thread across product development and supply chain collaboration that removes blind spots, accelerates decisions, and builds partner trust—grounding choices in data, avoiding compliance surprises, and creating measurable time-to-market gains.

[An image that shows the unified inbox and a person in front of a laptop.] 

The centralized task center consolidates approvals and forms across PLM, procurement, and internal collaboration, giving processors a single inbox to claim, complete, and submit. Each task carries its linked objects and documentation, so decisions happen in context. Process inputs like best-before dates, delivery dates, specification attributes become first-class variables that follow the workflow instance. This structure enables targeted routing, SLA measurement, and proactive nudges without burdening contributors. The outcome is one operational surface for multi-system processes with built-in traceability.

[An image that shows the task center UI.]

3. Monitor KPIs and Act Proactively

Visibility must be designed. The Process Visibility Dashboard aggregates all running workflow instances into an interactive, graphical view. Teams can combine multiple workflows, define relationships via shared business keys, and expose the dimensions that matter: status, cycle time, release counts, and any domain-specific date or attribute. With those dimensions in place, the organization defines performance indicators and sets reactive or proactive actions. If a best-before date is surpassed or a cycle-time benchmark is at risk, actions trigger automatically: start a corrective workflow, send a targeted notification, or block a release. This closes the loop from monitoring to response and turns dashboards into operational control.

[An image that shows the Collaboration overview in the dashboard.]

 The result: an estimated 35% reduction in cycle time.

What's Coming Next?

Prompt-to-Process

  • Describe your workflow in natural language and let AI generate the initial flow

AI Form Generation

  • Automate the creation of complex business forms via simple text instructions

Task Validation Agent [Roadmap]

  • Validate Correctness and Completeness within workflow tasks

 

[An image that shows the use of the AI agent.]

On the roadmap is a capability that takes AI a step further. The Task Validation Agent automatically compares a submitted task – an uploaded certificate, a completed feasibility document – against its expected outcome, delivering an instant correctness and completeness score with specific recommendations. Users get a first-level screening before they even submit, reducing review cycles and accelerating throughput.

[An image that shows the use of the AI agent.]

Conclusion

End-to-end process visibility turns reactive status-chasing into proactive, data-driven operations. SAP Build Process Automation, integrated with SAP IPD, connects workflow execution, task management, and KPI monitoring into a single auditable system – with AI making it faster and smarter at every step.

 

More Information

  •  SAP Supply Chain Management Enablement Hub: https://support.sap.com/en/product/supply-chain-management/enablement.html
  • SAP Integrated Product Development on SAP Community: https://community.sap.com/topics/integrated-product-development
  • SAP Build Process Automation on SAP Community: https://community.sap.com/topics/build-process-automation
  • SAP Task Center Documentation: https://help.sap.com/docs/task-center
  • SAP Integrated Product Development - Roadmap: Roadmap Explorer

 

Connect with us

  • Get engaged: To schedule a Value Discovery Workshop, contact: ayan.pandey@sap.com [Product Manager, SAP IPD]

 

Don't hesitate to leave a comment and check out our community page to ask your questions and engage with the experts. Follow the PSCC_Enablement tag to stay up to date with our latest blog posts. 

Follow me on LinkedIn: https://www.linkedin.com/in/annette-garcia-lorenzo

entry.title: What's New on the SAP Analytics Cloud Home Screen — Q2 2026 QRC: The New Blog Card
entry.content:
The Blog Card is a new card coming to the SAP Analytics Cloud Home Screen in the Q2 2026 QRC release:

What it is:

A dedicated card that surfaces the 10 most recent SAP Analytics Cloud blog posts from the SAP Community, directly on your Home Screen. Each entry shows the title, author, publish date, and a teaser. Click any post to open the full article in a new tab.

 

What you can do:

Pin blog posts you want to keep at the top of the card for quick access.

 

You can also show or hide the card from your Home Screen at any time.

Why it matters:

No more navigating to the SAP Community separately to stay up to date. The latest SAC product news, how-tos, and community insights appear automatically, right where you start your day.

Check your Home Screen after the release and start exploring!

entry.title: Re: SAP Analytics Cloud to S4HANA Live Connection CDS vie...
entry.content:
Hello @Parag_Savarkar 

 

In SAC Live connection (S/4HANA):

You will only see CDS views (queries) you are authorized for in S/4HANA .
Authorization is controlled by S/4HANA roles (e.g., analytic privileges / CDS access control) .

You have access only to OTC views in S/4HANA
You will only see those 2 OTC views in SAC
RTR and STP views will not be visible at all (not even with empty data)


No access = view not visible
Access but restricted data = visible with filtered data

 

If you want all 6 views visible, you must get authorization for RTR & STP CDS views in S/4HANA .


Thanks,
Sravan

entry.title: Re: CDS views to create an Extern API in SAP Public cloud...
entry.content:
Hello @Gahan_Narayan1 

C_SALESCHANGEDOCITEMDEX is a consumption CDS cannot be used directly for API (OData/custom CDS).

1. Use underlying tables/CDS
Use change log tables:
`CDHDR` (header)
`CDPOS` (items)
Create your own CDS + expose as OData

2. Check for interface CDS (I\_\ )
If available, use interface views (I\_\ ) , not C\_\

3. Reuse standard API (recommended)
Use SAP standard APIs for Sales Order + change logs (if available in API Hub)

4. Fallback (not ideal)
BADI/ABAP SELECT (as you mentioned)



Build custom CDS on `CDHDR/CDPOS` → expose via Custom OData API (SEGW / RAP)

 

Thanks,
Sravan

entry.title: What “Dedicated Partnership” Really Means: Inside the Max Success Plan Experience for SAP SuccessFac
entry.content:
What “Dedicated Partnership” Really Means: Inside the Max Success Plan Experience for SAP SuccessFactors

When customers first hear “dedicated partnership” in the context of Max Success Plan, the reaction is often mixed: some expect a named individual or an escalation‑only relationship. Others assume it means faster answers, more meetings, or privileged access to support. And some are simply unsure what it translates to in practice, beyond a premium label.

The reality is more nuanced, and definitely more practical: a dedicated partnership under Max Success Plan is not about standing above the transformation. It’s about being embedded in it, across decisions, phases, and moments where complexity makes the right path difficult to see.

This article looks inside that experience, giving you a point of view that will hopefully colour the picture of what we mean by dedicated partnership in Max Success Plan context.

The Question Customers Usually Ask (Even If They Don’t Say It)

Once organisations reach the scale where Max Success Plan becomes relevant, they often start with a question: who is actually helping us navigate the choices that don’t have a clear right answer? Because they already know SAP SuccessFactors and how it works. What they really want to know is:

  • Should this decision be made now, or deferred?
  • Are we solving a local need, or creating a global precedent?
  • Does this “temporary” fix introduce future risk?
  • Have we thought far enough ahead to understand downstream impact?

These questions don’t show up neatly in support tickets or project plans. More often, they surface in workshops where trade‑offs are debated, in steering committees where timelines and risks collide, in release discussions when priorities don’t align, or during cross‑team escalations where ownership is unclear.

What makes these moments challenging is not a lack of expertise, but the lack of shared context: decisions are being made under pressure, by different groups, each with partial visibility of the broader landscape. By the time something becomes a formal issue, the decision has usually already been taken, and its impact is already spreading downstream.

This is where a dedicated partnership in Max Success Plan makes a practical difference: instead of engaging after decisions are locked in, Max experts are present in those exact moments of uncertainty, helping frame the question correctly, surface dependencies early, and bring architectural, operational, and transformation‑level context into the discussion before a path is chosen. The partnership operates alongside customers while decisions are still forming, not retrospectively when options are limited.

When you see the partnership working this way, it becomes clear that “dedicated” is not about availability or hierarchy, it’s about proximity to decision‑making. It’s about having the right expertise present while trade‑offs are being discussed, not retrospectively analysing them once consequences are already playing out.

That shift - from episodic involvement to continuous context - is what changes the customer experience under Max. And it’s best understood not in abstract terms, but in the way the partnership shows up day to day.

What “Dedicated” Looks Like in Practice

Max Success Plan customers don’t just receive services; they gain ongoing access to recognised HCM experts (i.e. Solution Architects, Coaches, and Experts‑on‑Demand) who engage across the full SAP SuccessFactors lifecycle, from early discovery through long‑term operation and continuous change.

What makes this experience fundamentally different is not seniority or volume of interaction, but continuity of involvement. Instead of engaging SAP expertise only at predefined milestones or when a specific issue arises, Max establishes an ongoing relationship in which context is deliberately built, maintained, and reused over time.

These experts understand not only the current initiative being discussed, but also the decisions that came before it, the constraints under which those decisions were made, and the direction the organisation is trying to move next. They are aware of the architectural choices already in place, the trade‑offs that have been accepted, and the areas where flexibility still exists. That accumulated understanding means guidance does not start from zero each time a new question emerges.

As a result, conversations change: rather than reacting to symptoms after they appear, guidance becomes anticipatory; potential conflicts are surfaced earlier; dependencies are highlighted before they turn into blockers; and decision‑makers are able to see the downstream impact of their choices while there are still options available.

Over time, this continuity reduces friction across initiatives, lowers the need for repeated re‑alignment, and gives customers a stable reference point as their SAP SuccessFactors landscape continues to evolve.

When continuity of context is established, something subtle but important changes in how customers experience SAP involvement. The engagement stops being centred around resolving visible problems and starts to influence how decisions are made before those problems exist. This is where customers begin to notice that they are spending less time reacting,  and more time thinking ahead.

Less Firefighting, More Foresight

In many large HR programmes, SAP expertise is traditionally brought in when something feels risky, blocked, or close to escalation. By the time that happens, the organisation is usually already deep into a path that was chosen under pressure. Options are limited, timelines are tight, and the focus shifts to mitigating impact rather than shaping outcomes.

A Max engagement changes that dynamic by shifting when and how experts are involved: instead of stepping in only at points of friction, Max experts are engaged earlier, often at the stage where questions are still being framed and decisions are not yet locked in. This might be during early architecture discussions, integration design conversations, global‑local governance debates, data strategy reviews, or exploratory discussions around AI and new capabilities.

The practical effect is not an increase in meetings or oversight, it is a redistribution of effort. Time that would otherwise be spent later on remediation, explanation, or rework is invested earlier in stress‑testing assumptions, surfacing dependencies, and considering downstream impact.

Customers often describe the impact not as receiving “more help,” but as exercising better judgement under pressure: decisions feel more deliberate, trade‑offs are clearer. Risks are addressed when they are still manageable rather than when they have already solidified into constraints. This foresight reduces escalation intensity and creates a sense that the transformation is being steered.

Partnership Across the Whole Transformation, Not Just Projects

Max Success Plan is explicitly structured to span the full SAP SuccessFactors lifecycle: Discover → Prepare → Explore → Realise → Deploy → Run. In practical terms, this means the partnership does not pause when a project phase ends, when priorities temporarily shift, or when operational realities take precedence over transformation work.

This continuity becomes especially valuable during transitions, moments where context is most often lost:

  • When responsibility moves from project teams to operational teams
  • when one implementation partner is replaced by another
  • or when an initial rollout gives way to ongoing change and optimisation, the transformation narrative normally fragments
  • decisions made earlier are revisited without full awareness of why they were taken, and governance has to be rebuilt from memory rather than intent.

Under Max Succes Plan, those handovers look different. Instead of resetting context at every transition, the same architectural understanding, decision rationale, and transformation goals continue to inform the next phase. This makes governance more consistent, reduces re‑litigation of past decisions, and allows organisations to evolve without repeatedly destabilising themselves.

At this point, many customers naturally ask a follow‑up question: if SAP is this embedded across the lifecycle, how does that interact with internal teams and partners?

What a Max Partnership Is – and What It is Not

A Max Success Plan partnership is not an override of your implementation partner. It does not sit above delivery teams issuing instructions or changing scope. It is also not a replacement for day‑to‑day operational support, nor does it remove ownership from internal HR or IT teams.

Instead, SAP Max Success Plan introduces something more subtle and often more valuable: an independent, experienced perspective whose sole responsibility is to safeguard the success of the transformation as a whole.

This perspective exists alongside customers and partners, not in competition with them. Its role is to help ensure that decisions made in one context do not unintentionally undermine outcomes in another, and that local progress does not erode global integrity over time. By maintaining transformation‑level visibility, Max helps align delivery, operations, and innovation around a shared intent, even as teams, timelines, and priorities change.

For customers, this clarity is what makes the partnership feel supportive rather than intrusive: it strengthens accountability without slowing delivery, and it creates confidence that the transformation is being guided, not just executed.

Is This the Conversation You’re Ready to Have?

If your SAP SuccessFactors environment is becoming more strategic, more interconnected, and harder to change without ripple effects, then the value of Max Success Plan is rarely in a single service, it’s in the quality of partnership.

The best next step is a conversation with your Customer Success Manager or Account Executive about whether your organisation would benefit from that level of engagement, not because progress is stalled, but because the cost of getting key decisions wrong has increased.

entry.title: Re: Introducing Expanded Usage of the Reference Capability Model
entry.content:
@lorimer1 Hi Rob, Thanks for your valuable feedback.  

We clarify that the term “Reference Capability Model” is used as an umbrella term for BCM and TCM, which are currently still two separate structures, with only BCM exposed to LeanIX. In particular, in SAP Roadmap Explorer, no differentiation is made between Business Capabilities and Technology Capabilities. We are also evaluating a true merge (while respecting the MECE principles) and plan to share this within the CEI to collect feedback in the coming months. Thanks !

entry.title: Re: Extension ledger configuration
entry.content:
Hi BilalHashmi,

yes, you can add extension ledgers after you have activated your standard ledger(s) in your productive system. You can even have several extension ledgers that are based on the standard ledger (in your case 0L). Just be aware that the extension ledger inherits the settings of the underlying standard ledger, so no differing accounting principles can be maintained, for example.

You can find more information on the SAP Help Portal:

Extension Ledger 

Extension Ledger Types

And for a comprehensive link collection on ledger configuration, you can visit the SAP Community page Ledger Configuration | SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition Finance | SAP Community.

I hope this helps.

Best regards,
Verena
